agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"An established logistics system enforces a strict 1:1 Shipment-to-Carrier assignment, with supervisor-only reassignment. The product team requests an auto-split feature that would allow shipments over 500 kg to be divided among multiple carriers without supervisor approval, directly conflicting with two foundational domain rules.","bounded_context":"Shipment Fulfillment / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of freight that must have exactly one assigned Carrier at all times from assignment through dispatch."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The entity responsible for transporting a Shipment. Exactly one Carrier is assigned per Shipment."},{"term":"Sub-shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept: a child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment that exceeds a weight threshold. Each sub-shipment would be a full Shipment in its own right with its own Carrier."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment Lock","definition":"The invariant that once a Carrier is assigned, only a Supervisor can reassign. Prevents unauthorized mid-process changes."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role that is the sole authority permitted to change Carrier assignments after initial assignment."},{"term":"Auto-split","definition":"Proposed feature to automatically decompose an overweight Shipment into multiple sub-shipments, each with a different Carrier, bypassing the supervisor gate."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Every Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier at all times from assignment through dispatch.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a data integrity constraint — a Shipment without exactly one Carrier is in a corrupt state. Accountability, insurance, chain-of-custody, and billing all depend on a single responsible Carrier per Shipment.","testable_assertion":"For any Shipment in status >= ASSIGNED, COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization integrity constraint. Unauthorized reassignment breaks chain-of-custody, audit trail, and liability tracking. Removing it corrupts the trust model.","testable_assertion":"Any Carrier reassignment event must have an associated Supervisor actor ID; reassignment attempts without Supervisor authority are rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"The auto-split feature requests that Shipments exceeding 500 kg be automatically split into multiple sub-shipments, each with a different Carrier, without Supervisor involvement.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 500 kg threshold is a business policy that could be changed by product leadership. However, the bypass of Supervisor authority and multi-carrier assignment are what conflict with existing invariants.","testable_assertion":"When a Shipment weight > 500 kg, the system creates N sub-shipments where each sub-shipment weight <= 500 kg and each has exactly one Carrier."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A Shipment with weight exceeding 500 kg is submitted","when":"The auto-split process executes","then":"The original Shipment is decomposed into N sub-shipments, each weighing <= 500 kg, and each sub-shipment has exactly one assigned Carrier (preserving BR-1)."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A Shipment is split into sub-shipments","when":"Any sub-shipment's Carrier needs to change after assignment","then":"A Supervisor must authorize the reassignment (preserving BR-2)."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A Shipment is split into sub-shipments","when":"The split completes","then":"The parent Shipment retains a traceable link to all child sub-shipments for audit, billing, and chain-of-custody purposes."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A Shipment weighing exactly 500 kg or less","when":"The auto-split process evaluates it","then":"No split occurs; the Shipment proceeds with its single Carrier assignment unchanged."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A Shipment weighing 501 kg — just over the threshold. Splitting into two sub-shipments of ~250 kg each may be operationally wasteful.","expected_behavior":"Business must define whether there is a minimum sub-shipment weight or whether marginal overages should be handled differently (e.g., a tolerance band)."},{"scenario":"A Carrier is already assigned to the parent Shipment before auto-split triggers. The assignment lock (BR-2) means only a Supervisor can change it.","expected_behavior":"The auto-split process either must be treated as a Supervisor-level action, or the split must occur BEFORE initial Carrier assignment — the timing in the workflow is critical."},{"scenario":"A Shipment is split, and one sub-shipment fails delivery while others succeed.","expected_behavior":"The parent Shipment cannot be marked fully delivered. Partial delivery status and reconciliation logic must be defined."},{"scenario":"Weight is revised after split (e.g., actual weight differs from declared weight at weigh station).","expected_behavior":"Unclear whether sub-shipments should be re-split, merged, or manually adjusted. Needs domain decision."},{"scenario":"Circular split: a sub-shipment itself exceeds 500 kg due to indivisible cargo.","expected_behavior":"Must define whether recursive splitting is allowed or whether indivisible items over 500 kg are exempt and require manual handling."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Multi-carrier shipments increase coordination costs, split billing complexity, and potential disputes over liability. However, they may unlock the ability to handle larger shipments that were previously rejected or delayed.","operational":"Introducing sub-shipments is a significant domain model expansion. Every downstream system — tracking, billing, proof-of-delivery, insurance, SLA monitoring — must understand the parent-child relationship. This is not a simple feature addition; it is a domain model evolution.","user":"Customers expect a single tracking number and unified delivery experience. Splitting into sub-shipments must be transparent to the end customer or clearly communicated. Supervisors lose a control point, which may affect operational oversight.","risk":"HIGH. The feature as described directly violates two production invariants. Implementing it without resolving the contradictions risks data corruption (shipments with ambiguous carrier ownership), liability gaps (no single accountable carrier for the parent shipment), and audit trail breakage.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their exclusive authority over carrier reassignment is bypassed for auto-split shipments. This undermines their operational control and may have compliance implications."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"Carriers now share responsibility for what was previously a single shipment. Liability, SLA accountability, and payment terms must be redefined."},{"group":"Billing/Finance","impact":"Splitting a shipment means splitting invoices, reconciling partial deliveries, and handling disputes across multiple carriers for a single customer order."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Must be shielded from internal splitting complexity or given clear visibility into sub-shipment tracking. Partial delivery scenarios affect customer satisfaction."},{"group":"Compliance/Legal","impact":"Chain-of-custody for a single shipment is now distributed. Regulatory requirements for cargo tracking may not permit this without explicit documentation."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The feature request directly contradicts two established production invariants (1:1 carrier assignment and supervisor-only reassignment). Until the product team clarifies whether they intend to relax these invariants or introduce sub-shipments as first-class domain entities that individually preserve them, the spec is internally contradictory. The 500 kg threshold and split mechanics are secondary concerns compared to the fundamental model conflict.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction between auto-split multi-carrier assignment and the existing 1:1 Carrier-per-Shipment invariant is clear and unambiguous","The bypass of Supervisor authority directly conflicts with the existing reassignment lock","Sub-shipments as first-class Shipments would preserve BR-1 if each gets its own Carrier"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends sub-shipments to be full Shipment entities or a new lightweight concept","Whether the 500 kg threshold accounts for indivisible cargo","The intended workflow timing — does split happen before or after initial Carrier assignment"],"assumptions":["The existing domain model and invariants are correctly described and actively enforced in production","The product team has not yet considered the impact on the existing invariant model","Sub-shipments as independent Shipments (each with one Carrier) would be an acceptable resolution path"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature requires assigning multiple carriers to a single shipment, directly violating the production invariant that every Shipment must have exactly one Carrier. The feature cannot be implemented as described without either (a) breaking the invariant or (b) introducing sub-shipments as first-class Shipment entities that each individually satisfy the invariant.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Team","Supervisors","Carriers","Billing/Finance","Compliance/Legal"],"options":["Introduce sub-shipments as first-class Shipment entities, each with exactly one Carrier, preserving BR-1. The parent Shipment becomes a composite/aggregate with no direct Carrier assignment.","Relax the 1:1 Carrier invariant system-wide to allow multi-carrier Shipments — HIGH RISK, affects all existing logic.","Require Supervisor approval for auto-split (preserving BR-2) while introducing sub-shipments (resolving the multi-carrier issue via option A)."],"recommendation":"Option A (sub-shipments as first-class Shipments) combined with Option C (Supervisor approval for the split action). This preserves both invariants: each sub-shipment has one Carrier, and the split itself is a Supervisor-authorized action. The auto part can be a Supervisor-approved policy rule rather than a full bypass."},{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature explicitly bypasses Supervisor involvement, directly contradicting the invariant that only a Supervisor can change Carrier assignments. Even if sub-shipments resolve the multi-carrier issue, the original Shipment's Carrier assignment is effectively voided without Supervisor authorization when it is decomposed.","affected_stakeholders":["Supervisors","Compliance/Legal"],"options":["Require Supervisor approval for each auto-split event, making it supervisor-assisted rather than fully automatic.","Create a new 'Auto-Split Policy' that a Supervisor pre-approves (e.g., all shipments over 500 kg from warehouse X are pre-authorized for splitting), preserving Supervisor authority at the policy level.","Bypass Supervisor authority for splits only — carve out an exception to BR-2 specifically for system-initiated splits."],"recommendation":"Option B — a pre-approved Auto-Split Policy. Supervisors retain authority by defining the conditions under which splits occur. The system executes automatically within those Supervisor-defined boundaries. This preserves the spirit of BR-2 while enabling the automation the product team wants."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The feature as described assigns multiple carriers to a single shipment without introducing sub-shipments as separate entities.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: Every Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier."},{"violation":"The feature bypasses Supervisor involvement for what is effectively a Carrier reassignment (the original Carrier is replaced by multiple new Carriers across sub-shipments).","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier."}]}
